narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background includes upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which are common among applicants. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 sits a bit below the typical 3.21 average, while your LSAT score of 29 is notably lower than the average of around 37. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 79.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average of about 5.5—which strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again well under the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about consistent early academic performance. Your family income bracket, while upper-middle and more favorable than many, provided a modest counterbalance by slightly nudging the prediction toward success, though not enough to offset the academic red flags. Compared to typical students who get approved, your low decile rankings stand out as unusually weak, while most successful applicants rank in the middle or higher deciles with stronger test scores. This combination made your profile riskier in the model's eyes than the average applicant. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 35 or above, and focus on improving your academic deciles through stronger performance in any remaining coursework or targeted prep programs. Many students in similar situations, including Hispanic applicants, have succeeded by addressing these gaps and reapplying after building a more competitive record. Your full-time commitment is a positive, so lean into that while seeking mentorship or bar prep resources early. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for future applications.
